item.title: The new chair effect
item.description: First impressions, priced in

item.title: Markets get the measure of Trump
item.description: At the very first sign that the president is buckling on an unorthodox foreign policy decision, that is the time to pounce

item.title: Europe’s stocks offer a peace dividend their US peers can’t match
item.description: European companies have more to gain by way of recovery if energy shortages caused by the Iran conflict ease

item.title: Rathbones’ blunder boosts banks in the race for the UK’s rich
item.description: An internal review that found regulatory compliance shortcomings sent the stock down 17%

item.title: Harry Potter has ruined Britain
item.description: Our greatest assets have been appropriated by the Ministry of Wizardry

item.title: ChatGPT moved my cheese: AI is unsettling the self-help shelf
item.description: Instant summaries sound the death knell for the bullet-point books that prey on our insecurities

item.title: Why dignity is the measure that matters
item.description: From litter-pickers to cage fighters, the signs of a society’s health are often hidden in plain sight

item.title: Take our phones too! Let’s add adults to the social media ban
item.description: We have to face up to the fact that we’re all addicted to online scrolling

item.title: The window for peace in Ukraine won’t be open forever
item.description: There is an opportunity to freeze the conflict, but Putin’s fantasy of total victory could get in the way

item.title: The cooler corner of Côtes du Rhône
item.description: In an under-sung swathe of the prolific appellation, small-scale, independent producers are crafting fresh, quality wines — and raising expectations

item.title: Why are we not talking to one another?
item.description: Conversations with a chatbot will never provide the same human nourishment

item.title: Should I pay off my child’s student loans?
item.description: A cash gift may be better used elsewhere, but be wary of inheritance tax implications

item.title: UK investors need to stop buying rubbish stocks
item.description: Retail investors who back the wrong companies for the wrong reasons are helping to kill the London market

item.title: Cue Point, London: Low-and-slow Texan technique meets the fragrant flavours of Afghanistan — restaurant review
item.description: After a decade of pop-ups and the festival circuit, the award-winning smokehouse delights at a new permanent home in Notting Hill

item.title: Britain needs a Labour contest, not a coronation
item.description: Andy Burnham should be tested on his policies against other leadership contenders

item.title: ‘Beware small states’: why Lebanon endangers the unfinished Israel-Iran war
item.description: The country carries the seeds of escalation that could undo Trump’s tenuous deal with Tehran

item.title: The Knicks’ victory points to the value of scarcity
item.description: The NBA team’s value continues to rise as investors chase a limited number of assets

item.title: Andy Burnham will need to play a new card now
item.description: His winning campaign in Makerfield was long on hope but a vague prospectus won’t be enough in Number 10

item.title: Medallia’s collapse turns private credit into a private equity problem
item.description: Private lenders that once fancied themselves as ‘coupon clippers’ had better be prepared to run companies as equity owners

item.title: Don’t blame passive investors if SpaceX is mispriced
item.description: Index funds don’t misallocate capital — that’s what stock pickers do

item.title: Why don’t we take Father’s Day seriously?
item.description: Even dads don’t care about it — so they say…

item.title: Reaching a nuclear deal with Iran will be much harder than in 2015
item.description: Tehran’s position has hardened and trust has declined in the years since the JCPOA

item.title: A reprieve for gilts
item.description: Plus Asian inflation

item.title: Nigel Farage should be worried
item.description: Fear of a Reform victory led Makerfield voters — including some 2024 Conservatives — to tactically vote Labour

item.title: Are insurers becoming dangerously addicted to private credit ratings?
item.description: We should worry about what happens when regulatory arbitrage goes unchecked
